source: All-In Podcast — E274: SpaceX $2T case, Nvidia shock selloff
summary: Orbital/space-based compute is real and will hit SpaceX's bottom line in second half of 2028 to first half of 2030
summary_extended: Baker argues that orbital/space-based compute will become a commercial reality on SpaceX's P&L between H2 2028 and H1 2030, representing a significant new revenue stream for the company. He supports this view by pointing to a working H100 GPU already operating in space today — noting that Andrej Karpathy has trained a model on it — as evidence that the technical barriers are being overcome faster than the market appreciates. A key underappreciated competitive advantage Baker highlights is SpaceX's ability to use non-radiation-hardened commercial chips in space because their hardware happens to pass radiation testing, dramatically lowering the cost and complexity of deploying compute in orbit versus traditional space-grade hardware. The primary investment implication is bullish on SpaceX (private), with no clear publicly-traded direct beneficiary that Baker specifies — NVDA is mentioned only as the manufacturer of the H100 already in orbit, not as a structural beneficiary of this thesis.
detail: Baker gives a specific point prediction of H2 2028 to H1 2030 for orbital compute to be a commercial reality on SpaceX's P&L. He notes there is already a working H100 GPU in space today (Andrej Karpathy trained a model on it). SpaceX's ability to use non-radiation-hardened chips in space (they just happen to pass rad testing) is a key underappreciated competitive advantage.
confidence_evidence: Baker gives a specific timeline prediction but acknowledges uncertainty; calls it a 'point prediction'
quote_fragment: second half of 28 to first half of 2030 would be my point prediction

ticker_audit.reasoning: NVDA is referenced only because an H100 GPU (NVDA's product) is already in orbit — Baker does not argue that orbital compute creates a material incremental demand driver for NVDA specifically, nor does he frame NVDA as a beneficiary of this thesis. The thesis is about SpaceX's P&L opportunity, and NVDA's mention is incidental to illustrating that commercial chips can work in space.
